programs.name: Medical Assistance

eligibility.income_limits: For elderly (65+) under Non-MAGI categories: Single nursing home applicant under $2,982/month (2026); individual applicants $2,901/month (2025). Varies by category (MAGI vs Non-MAGI), marital status, and household size. MAGI adults 19-64: 133%-138% FPL. No full household table available; limits compared to Federal Poverty Guidelines (FPIG) with 5% disregard for some groups.[1][2][4][5]
eligibility.asset_limits: Non-MAGI elderly/disabled: $2,000 single, $3,000 couple. Long-term care: $2,400 (income >$2,901/month), $8,000 (income <$2,901/month). Counts: wages, interest, dividends, Social Security, pensions, spouse income. Exemptions not detailed; resources assessed in some cases only.[1][2][3][7]
other_requirements: Pennsylvania residency (no minimum time, intent to stay)
U.S. citizen, refugee, or qualified non-citizen (proof required)
Social Security Number (assistance available if none)
Identity verification (driver's license, state ID)
For long-term care: Nursing Facility Level of Care (NFLOC) or functional need in ADLs
Blind/disabled or receiving LTC/HCBS for Non-MAGI

benefits.value: Comprehensive health care coverage including doctor visits, hospital care, prescriptions, long-term care (nursing home, HCBS waivers), home modifications if qualified. Specifics vary by category; no fixed dollar/hour amounts stated.[1][2][8]

methods: Online: COMPASS.state.pa.us
In-person or paper: Local County Assistance Offices (CAO)
Mail: To local CAO
application.processing_time: Lengthy and challenging; no specific timeline given. Medical assessment and financial review follow submission.[2][7]
application.waitlist: Not mentioned; may vary by long-term care services[2]
forms: Medical Assistance application via COMPASS or CAO; no specific form number listed
documents: Proof of identity (driver's license, state ID)
Social Security Number
Proof of citizenship/immigration status
Income verification (paystubs, Social Security statements, pensions)
Asset/resource documentation
Proof of residency (lease, utility bill)
Medical/functional need evidence for LTC (assessment arranged post-application)

geography.regional_variations: Administered by local County Assistance Offices (CAO); potential differences in processing, wait times, providers by county. Medical assessments via Independent Enrollment Broker (IEB).[3][5][7]

gotchas: Separate MAGI (expansion, income-only) vs Non-MAGI (elderly/LTC with assets) rules; elderly typically Non-MAGI with stricter asset tests
Long-term care requires NFLOC; application lengthy with documentation
Income/assets change annually; 2025/2026 figures differ slightly across sources
Spousal income counted; Medicaid planning needed if over limits
Annual renewal required; immigrants need 5-year qualified status in some cases
No SSI counting in MAGI but does in Non-MAGI
programs.data_shape_notes: Dual tracks (MAGI income-only vs Non-MAGI income+assets); elderly/LTC focused on Non-MAGI with NFLOC; county-administered with annual FPL-based limits varying by marital status and program subcategory

programs.name: Community HealthChoices

eligibility.income_limits: Monthly income limit is 300% of the Federal Benefit Rate (FBR), which adjusts annually. In 2025, up to $2,901 per applicant regardless of marital status; each spouse considered individually if both apply. Older data (2019) shows $2,313[2][6].
eligibility.asset_limits: Countable resources at or below $2,000 for an individual after a $6,000 standard disregard (2019 data; confirm current). Home equity limit of $730,000 (2025) if applicant lives in home or intends to return, or if non-applicant spouse/dependent resides there. Countable assets include bank accounts, property, retirement accounts; exemptions include primary home under equity limit, certain personal items[2][6][8].
other_requirements: Pennsylvania resident

Medicaid eligible (dually eligible for Medicare/Medicaid, or qualify for Medicaid LTSS needing nursing facility level of care via Functional Eligibility Determination (FED) assessing ADLs like bathing, dressing, eating)

Not eligible if: intellectual/developmental disabilities receiving ODP services beyond supports coordination, resident in state-operated nursing facility (including veterans' homes), or certain OBRA/Act 150 participants without NFLOC or dual eligibility
Exception: 55+ may opt for LIFE program in served areas instead[1][2][3][4][5][6][8]

benefits.value: Up to 32 services including attendant care, adult day services, home modifications, pest control, educational support, personal assistance, respite, and other LTSS to support community living at nursing facility level of care. Medicare benefits continue separately if dual eligible[1][3].

methods: Phone: Pennsylvania Independent Enrollment Broker (PA IEB) at 1-877-550-4227
Online: COMPASS website
County Assistance Office (CAO) for financial eligibility (submit PA-600 form)
In-person: Local Area Agency on Aging (AAA) for needs assessment; CAO for financial review
application.processing_time: Not specified in sources; involves sequential steps including assessment and review
forms: PA-600 for financial eligibility at CAO
documents: Proof of income, assets, insurance coverage
Bank statements, property details, retirement accounts
Medicare/Medicaid info
ID/residency proof

geography.restrictions: Available statewide except exclusions like state-operated facilities; LIFE alternative for 55+ in provider-served areas only
geography.regional_variations: Managed by different Managed Care Organizations (MCOs); needs assessment by local Area Agency on Aging (AAA); potential regional differences in providers, wait times, CAO processing
offices_or_providers: Managed Care Organizations (choose after approval, e.g., UPMC)

gotchas: Mandatory managed care program; must choose MCO post-approval
Income/assets reviewed strictly by CAO; spousal rules treat each applicant individually
Functional need requires NFLOC via FED tool—not just age/Medicaid
Excludes IDD via ODP, state nursing homes; prior waiver enrollees auto-transitioned
LIFE opt-in for 55+ may be preferable in served areas
Home equity limit applies if intending return
Medicare handled separately[1][2][4][5][6]
programs.data_shape_notes: Medicaid LTSS waiver with tiered services based on NFLOC; financial eligibility via CAO with individual applicant limits regardless of household; varies by MCO and local AAA; annual FBR adjustments

programs.name: PACE/PACENET

eligibility.income_limits: PACE: Single person $14,500 or less annually; married couple $17,700 or less annually. PACENET: Single person $14,500 to $33,500 annually; married couple $17,700 to $41,500 annually. Income based on previous calendar year. Applicants within $0.50 over limits may round down. Medicare Part B premiums excluded from income.[1][4][5]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits or test. Assets not counted.[6]
other_requirements: Pennsylvania resident for at least 90 consecutive days prior to application.[1][2][3]
Not receiving prescription benefits under Medical Assistance (MA). Those with only Medicare Part B premium help via Medicare Savings Program may qualify.[1][4]

Must report all previous year's income including gross Social Security/SSI (excluding Medicare premiums), pensions, wages, self-employment, alimony, annuities/IRAs, unemployment, interest/dividends/capital gains, net rental income, royalties, workers' comp, death benefits over $10,000, spouse's income if living together, cash gifts/inheritances over $300. Capital gains from home sale within 2 years must be declared even without tax filing. Exclusions: Medicare Part B, Property Tax/Rent Rebates, VA Aid & Attendance, SNAP/LIHEAP, first $10,000 death benefit, federal stimulus, etc.[6]

benefits.value: Prescription drug coverage (generic and brand-name) with co-pays. PACE: $6 generic, $9 brand-name per Rx; no monthly premium. PACENET: $8 generic, $15 brand-name per Rx; monthly premium ~$35.63 (2020 rate). Works with Medicare Part D, retiree/employer plans, Medicare Advantage, VA benefits to reduce out-of-pocket costs. Excludes experimental/OTC drugs, FDA DESI-listed unless 'medically necessary'.[1][5][7]

methods: Online: www.aging.pa.gov or https://pacecares.magellanhealth.com/.[1][4][7]
In-person/mail: Local pharmacy, senior center, Area Agency on Aging, district office.[1]
forms: PACE/PACENET application (available via phone, website, or pick-up locations). Specific form name/number not listed; PACE Application Center assists.[7]
documents: Proof of age, residency (90 days), previous year's income verification (e.g., Social Security/RRB1099, pension statements, tax returns, W-2s, 1099s). For nursing/personal care home: home agreement, last 2 invoices, cancelled checks/bank statements showing payments. Report home sale capital gains if within 2 years.[6]

geography.restrictions: None; available statewide for eligible PA residents.[1][7]
offices_or_providers: Administered by PA Department of Aging via PACE Application Center; local Area Agencies on Aging, pharmacies, senior centers for applications.[1][7]

gotchas: Income based strictly on prior calendar year; current year income irrelevant.[1][4]
Cannot receive if on MA prescription benefits; Medicare Part B help only is okay.[1][4]
Must declare all income sources including spouse's, capital gains from home sales (even no tax filing), cash gifts over $300.[6]
Limits set by State Lottery Law; no automatic annual adjustment (manual increases by legislature).[5]
Co-pays and premium may change; sources show 2020/2022 data, verify current.[5]
Drugs on FDA DESI list excluded unless physician notes 'medically necessary'.[3]
programs.data_shape_notes: Two-tier structure (PACE lower income/no premium; PACENET higher income/with premium); no asset test; income exclusions specific and detailed; statewide with local application points; funded by PA Lottery.

programs.name: Medicare Savings Program

eligibility.income_limits: 2026 limits (monthly) by category and household size: QMB (<100% FPL): Single $1,304, Couple $1,759; SLMB (<120% FPL): Single $1,565, Couple $2,111; QI-1 (<135% FPL): Single $1,760, Couple $2,373. Individuals may qualify with higher income/resources due to state disregards.[7][8]
eligibility.asset_limits: Resource limits (all categories): Single $9,950, Couple $14,910. Assets include real estate, mutual funds, stocks, bonds, retirement accounts, savings/checking (exemptions not detailed in sources; state may disregard certain types).[2][7][8]
other_requirements: Must have Medicare Part A (or qualify for Part B)
Pennsylvania resident
U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen
Meet identity, SSN, and residency verification

benefits.value: QMB: Full Medicare Part B premium (~$185/month in 2026), deductibles, coinsurance; no provider billing beneficiary. SLMB: Part B premium only. QI-1: Part B premium only. All categories cover Part B premium; QMB most comprehensive.[7][8]

methods: Online: COMPASS website (pa.gov/COMPASS)
Phone: 1-800-692-7462 (COMPASS)
Mail/In-person: Local County Assistance Office
documents: Proof of income
Proof of resources/assets
Medicare card/Part A proof
ID (driver's license/state ID)
SSN
Proof of citizenship/residency
Proof of residency in PA

geography.regional_variations: Administered via local County Assistance Offices; potential processing/wait time differences by county not detailed[6]

gotchas: Higher income/resources than table limits may still qualify due to DHS disregards[7][8]
Must apply even if not yet enrolled in Part B[7][8]
No resource exclusion for some categories like pregnant women/children, but MSP targets elderly[2]
QMB providers cannot bill beneficiary for Medicare-covered services[7][8]
Separate from PACE/PACENET prescription programs[6][9]
programs.data_shape_notes: Tiered by QMB/SLMB/QI-1 with escalating income limits; asset limits fixed by household size; state disregards allow flexibility beyond federal FPL

programs.name: SNAP

eligibility.income_limits: For households with a member 60+ or disabled, Pennsylvania uses expanded rules (Oct 1, 2025–Sept 30, 2026). Gross income limit at 200% federal poverty level: 1 person $2608/month, 2 $3526, 3 $4442, 4 $5358, 5 $6276, 6 $7192, 7 $8108, +$916 each additional. If over gross, qualify via net income and asset tests. Alternative 2025 senior limits cited: 1 person $15,060/year ($1255/month), 2 $20,440/year ($1703/month). Net income determines benefits after deductions (e.g., 30% of net from max allotment).[1][2][3]
eligibility.asset_limits: Applies if gross income >200% FPL; must meet net income + asset test. Exempt: home value, retirement savings, household goods, life insurance cash value (if not income-producing), income-producing property (varies by state). Most states waive asset test if income ≤ federal poverty line.[1][2]
other_requirements: Household must live in PA
U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen (note: Nov 1, 2025 changes restrict many immigrants; green card/Cuba/Haiti may still qualify)
SSN for all members
Work rules exempt for 60+ (but new Sep 1, 2025 rules for some adults under 60)

benefits.value: Monthly EBT card for food purchases. Average for older adults: $106/month. Calculated as max allotment minus 30% net income (e.g., 2-person elderly: $546 max - 30% net). Varies by net income, deductions (medical >$35 for 60+, shelter, utilities). Min/max allotments by household size.[1][4][5]

methods: Online: COMPASS website (pa.gov COMPASS tool)
Phone: County Assistance Office (e.g., senior helpline 1-877-999-5964)
In-person: County assistance office
Mail: Use Simple SNAP application form
application.processing_time: Not specified in sources; apply to check.
forms: Simple SNAP application (for households all 60+ or disabled, purchase/prepare food together)
documents: Dates of birth
Social Security numbers
Household income (SS, pensions, etc.)
Expenses (housing, medical >$35 for 60+, utilities)
Proof of shelter/utility costs (required as of Feb 9, 2026, if not on file; updates if move)

geography.restrictions: None; apply via county assistance office
geography.regional_variations: Administered by county offices; potential wait/processing differences not detailed

gotchas: Expanded PA rules looser than federal—check PA-specific limits
Medical/shelter deductions key for seniors to maximize benefits
New 2025/2026 changes: immigrant restrictions (Nov 1), work rules (Sep 1, exempt 60+), shelter/utility proof (Feb 9, 2026)
Include all who buy/prepare food
Assets exempt but test applies if high gross income
programs.data_shape_notes: benefits scale by household size; expanded gross limit (200% FPL) for 60+/disabled with net/asset fallback; high deductions for seniors (medical, shelter); county-administered with 2025-2026 federal changes

programs.name: LIHEAP

eligibility.income_limits: For the 2025-2026 heating season (effective through at least February 1, 2026), maximum annual gross income at or below 150% of Federal Poverty Guidelines: Household Size 1: $23,940; 2: $32,460; 3: $40,980; 4: $49,500; 5: $58,020; 6: $66,540; 7: $75,060; 8: $83,580; 9: $92,100; 10: $100,620. For each additional person, add $8,520. Income includes gross yearly earnings of all household members (related or unrelated, including roomers sharing expenses). Exclusions include certain educational assistance, home produce for consumption, Senior Citizen Rebate payments, and work/training incentives[1][2][4][7].
eligibility.asset_limits: No resource or asset limits apply[5].
other_requirements: Must reside in Pennsylvania
Renters and homeowners both eligible
No requirement to be on other public assistance
No requirement for unpaid heating bills
Household includes all children, adults, relatives, and unrelated roomers sharing expenses at the residence[1][2][4][5]

benefits.value: One-time cash grant of $200 to $1,000 paid directly to utility company or fuel provider. Exact amount determined by household size, income, fuel type, and county-specific benefit tables. Crisis grants available for households in immediate danger of being without heat. Grants are non-repayable[2][4][8].

methods: Online: COMPASS at https://www.compass.state.pa.us (Pennsylvania's online tool for health and human services)
Paper: Download form from county assistance office or DHS site, mail or deliver to local county assistance office
In-person: Local county assistance office (find via PA DHS site)
Phone: Contact local county assistance office (numbers via PA DHS LIHEAP page or 1-866-550-4355 for general inquiries, but apply via above methods)[1][2][4]
application.processing_time: Allow 30 days for response with written notice of eligibility and benefit amount[1].
application.waitlist: Not mentioned; applications accepted December 3, 2025 to May 8, 2026 for regular grants[2].
forms: LIHEAP application form (downloadable from county assistance office or COMPASS site)
documents: Names, birthdates, and Social Security Numbers (or lack thereof) for all household members
Proof of income for all household members (e.g., pay stubs, tax returns)
Most recent utility/heating bill
Proof of residence if needed[3]

geography.restrictions: Available to all Pennsylvania residents meeting eligibility; administered via local county assistance offices.
geography.regional_variations: Benefit amounts vary by county and fuel type via county-specific tables; different local offices handle intake and processing, potentially affecting wait times[1][8].
offices_or_providers: Local county assistance offices statewide (locate via https://www.pa.gov or COMPASS)[1][2]

gotchas: No age requirement—open to all qualifying households, not just elderly
Income limits updated annually (check current year; 2025-2026 figures apply)
Benefits vary significantly by county, fuel type (e.g., electric, gas, oil), income bracket, and household size—use official benefit table tool
Application window limited (Dec 3, 2025–May 8, 2026); apply early
No asset test, but all gross income sources count except specific exclusions
Crisis grants for emergencies separate from regular heating grants[2][4][5][8]
programs.data_shape_notes: Income at 150% FPL with annual updates; no asset test; benefits scale by household size, income range, county, and fuel type via lookup tables; seasonal application window; statewide but county-administered

programs.name: Weatherization Assistance Program

eligibility.income_limits: At or below 200% of the Federal Poverty Income Level (effective January 13, 2026). Examples: 1 person: $31,920 annually; 4 people: $66,000 annually. Full table from Monroe County (subject to annual updates): 7 people: $100,080; 8 people: $111,440; each additional: $11,360. Varies by household size; check current guidelines via local provider.
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned in program guidelines.
other_requirements: Pennsylvania resident
Occupy home as primary residence
Homeowners or renters (renters need landlord permission and rental agreement)
Eligible housing: single-family homes, rowhomes, trailers (no multi-family buildings unless 66% of units eligible, or 50% for duplexes/four-unit)
Priority for elderly, disabled, families with children, high energy users

benefits.value: Free energy efficiency improvements including: blower door air sealing; attic/wall/basement/crawlspace insulation and ventilation; heating system modification/replacement; minor repairs/health & safety measures. Average expenditure per household: $7,669 based on home audit. No repayment or liens required.

methods: Contact local weatherization agency serving your county (find via https://dced.pa.gov/programs/weatherization-assistance-program-wap/ or https://dced.pa.gov/housing-and-development/weatherization/income-eligibility/)
Phone examples: Monroe County 570-421-4300; Philadelphia (215) 448-2160
Email example: Philadelphia phdc.intake@phdc.phila.gov
In-person intake appointment with local provider
Referral via LIHEAP HSEA-1 form through local CAO if applying for related assistance
application.processing_time: Not specified statewide; involves energy audit after intake
application.waitlist: Active waitlists in some areas (e.g., Philadelphia); varies by region
documents: Name, birthdate, Social Security numbers for all household members
Photo ID of applicant
Proof of income for past 12 months for all members
Recent electric/fuel bills (past year usage)
Homeowners: tax bill, recorded deed, or mobile home title
Renters: landlord permission and rental agreement

geography.restrictions: Services administered by county-specific local agencies/providers
geography.regional_variations: Different local providers per county (e.g., Monroe County RDA, Philadelphia PHDC, York County Planning Commission); waitlists and intake processes vary (e.g., Philadelphia has active waitlist and email inquiries); contact county-specific agency for details
offices_or_providers: Find county provider via DCED site: https://dced.pa.gov/programs/weatherization-assistance-program-wap/

gotchas: Priority given to elderly/disabled/children/high energy users—may affect wait times
Renters need landlord permission; multi-family buildings have unit occupancy rules
No re-weatherization for 15 years unless damage from fire/flood/act of God
Must reapply annually to confirm eligibility
Only primary residences; housing type restrictions (no multi-family unless qualified)
Local waitlists common; processing varies by county demand
programs.data_shape_notes: Administered by county-specific providers with varying intake/wait times; income at exactly 200% FPL by household size; priority tiers influence service order; no statewide central application—must contact local agency

programs.name: SHIP

eligibility.income_limits: No strict income limits; prioritizes people with limited incomes, Medicare beneficiaries under 65 with disabilities, and dually eligible for Medicare/Medicaid. Exact thresholds vary; example for related Medicare Savings Programs: up to ~$1,800/month individual or ~$2,400/month couple[9].
eligibility.asset_limits: Not applicable; no asset test mentioned for core SHIP counseling services[7][10].
other_requirements: Medicare beneficiary or family/caregiver
Pennsylvania resident
Focus on those needing help with Medicare options, appeals, fraud prevention, or cost-saving programs like MSP/Extra Help[7][10]

benefits.value: Free one-on-one counseling, education, and assistance on Medicare (Parts A/B/C/D, Medigap), applying for low-income programs (Medicaid, MSP, Extra Help), appeals, rights/procedures, and preventing/detecting/reporting Medicare fraud via Senior Medicare Patrol (SMP). Outreach includes presentations, enrollment events, health fairs[7][10].

methods: Phone: Local SHIP site or 877-839-2675[7]
Website: shiphelp.org to find/partner with local site[7]
In-person: Over 2,200 local sites across PA via national network[7]
application.processing_time: Immediate counseling upon contact; no formal processing for services[7][10].
forms: No specific forms; provide Medicare info, income details if applying for cost-saving programs[7]
documents: Medicare card, income proof if seeking MSP/Extra Help eligibility assistance, citizenship/residency if relevant[7][9]

geography.regional_variations: Delivered via local sites/providers (over 2,200 nationally, including PA network); availability, team members (staff/volunteers), and events may vary by county/community[7].
offices_or_providers: Local SHIP counselors via shiphelp.org; PA-specific contacts through state Aging or DHS networks (not detailed in results)[7]

programs.official_source: https://www.shiphelp.org/ (national); PA details via acl.gov/programs or state aging sites[7]
gotchas: Not health insurance or direct financial aid—only free counseling/advocacy; confuses with CHIP (children's insurance)[1][2][7]
Must contact local site for PA-specific help; national numbers route locally[7]
Prioritizes limited-income/duals/disabled under 65—others still eligible but not prioritized[7]
Assists with MSP/Extra Help apps but families must qualify separately[9]
programs.data_shape_notes: Counseling/advocacy service via local network, no income/asset test for core help, prioritizes Medicare-related needs for elderly/families; distinct from CHIP (child health insurance)

programs.name: Home-Delivered Meals
programs._raw:
I cannot provide the comprehensive JSON guide you've requested because the search results do not contain the specific, detailed information needed to complete it accurately.

Here's what the search results reveal and what's missing:

## What the Search Results Show

**Eligibility varies significantly by county and program:**
- **Age requirement:** Consistently 60+ years old across all programs[1][2][6][7]
- **Core requirement:** Unable to safely prepare meals and/or shop for groceries[1][2][7]
- **Living situation:** Some programs require living alone or with someone who also qualifies[1]
- **Income limits:** Mentioned as existing[6][7][8] but no specific dollar amounts are provided in any search result

**Benefits are vague:**
- Meals are delivered to the home[1][2][6][7]
- One source mentions "five $5.00 checks" but this refers to a *different* program (Pennsylvania Senior Farmers Market Nutrition Program), not home-delivered meals[6]
- Frequency of delivery is not specified in most results
- One provider mentions "wellness checks" with meal delivery[8]

**Application process is fragmented:**
- York County: Call 717-771-9610 or email aging@yorkcountypa.gov; requires in-home assessment[1]
- Blair County: Call 946-1235[2]
- Montgomery County: No contact information provided[7]
- Delaware County: Through Senior Community Services[8]
- Philadelphia: Through Philadelphia Corporation for Aging[9]

## Critical Gaps

1. **No statewide income limits table** — Search results reference income eligibility but provide no dollar amounts
2. **No asset limits information** — Not mentioned in any result
3. **No processing timelines** — No waitlist information or approval timeframes
4. **No required documents list** — Not specified
5. **No unified application process** — Each county/provider operates independently with different contact methods
6. **No regional variation details** — Results show different providers by county but don't explain how services differ
7. **No benefit amounts** — Meal frequency, portion sizes, or dietary specifications are not quantified

## What You Actually Need

To build this guide accurately, you would need to:
- Contact the **Pennsylvania Department of Aging** directly for statewide policy[6]
- Reach out to each county's Area Agency on Aging for local income limits and application procedures
- Request specific program documentation from providers like Senior Community Services, Philadelphia Corporation for Aging, and Meals America

The program is **county-administered with significant local variation**, which means a single comprehensive guide may not be possible—you may need county-specific guides instead.

programs.name: Pennsylvania Caregiver Support Program

age.care_receiver: 60+ years, OR any age with Alzheimer's/related dementia (Category 1), OR under 18 (Category 2), OR 18-59 with disability (Category 3)

income_limits.reimbursement_eligibility: Care receiver household income up to 380% of Federal Poverty Level (FPL) receives financial reimbursement; income above 380% FPL receives non-financial services only (care management, education, training)
income_limits.note: Reimbursement percentage determined by sliding scale based on FPL and household size

eligibility.asset_limits: Not specified in search results for this program; however, if care receiver qualifies for Medicaid/Medical Assistance (separate program), 2026 limits are: $2,400 (single) or $4,800 (married couple both applying); excludes primary home (if equity ≤$752,000 and applicant/spouse/dependent lives there), one vehicle, household furnishings, personal effects
eligibility.residency: Both caregiver and care receiver must be Pennsylvania residents

relationship_requirements.Category_1: No relationship required; no cohabitation required
relationship_requirements.Category_2: Caregiver must be 55+, related by blood/marriage/adoption, have legal guardianship, or be raising child informally; must cohabitate
relationship_requirements.Category_3: Caregiver must be 55+, related by blood/marriage/adoption (can be biological parent); must cohabitate

other_requirements: Caregiver must be primary caregiver providing regular, hands-on care
Care receiver must require assistance with at least one activity of daily living (ADL)
In-home assessment required to verify needs and household income

financial_reimbursement.eligible_expenses: Reimbursement for expenses related to providing care (specific services not detailed in search results)
financial_reimbursement.sliding_scale: Based on care receiver household income and size; percentage varies

benefits.value: Specific dollar amounts per month not provided in search results; varies by household income and size

methods: Contact Area Agency on Aging (AAA) for your county — specific phone numbers and websites not provided in search results
Online: https://www.pa.gov/services/aging/apply-for-the-caregiver-support-program (referenced but full URL details not provided)
In-person: Area Agency on Aging office in your county
application.forms: Specific form names/numbers not provided in search results
documents: Verification of household income (required during in-home assessment)
Proof of Pennsylvania residency for both caregiver and care receiver
Documentation of care receiver's functional deficits or diagnosis (Alzheimer's/dementia/disability as applicable)
Proof of relationship (if applicable to category)

geography.restrictions: Statewide program; however, some regional variations exist through Area Agencies on Aging
geography.regional_variations: Southwestern PA (Fayette, Greene, Washington counties) has documented regional office; other counties have their own AAA offices. Eligibility criteria are statewide, but administration is regional.
geography.offices_or_providers: Administered through Area Agencies on Aging (AAA) by county; specific office locations not provided in search results

gotchas: No financial eligibility requirement exists, BUT reimbursement amount depends entirely on care receiver income — those above 380% FPL get services only, no money
Caregiver and care receiver do NOT need to be related in Category 1 (age 60+ or Alzheimer's), but MUST be related in Categories 2 & 3
Caregiver and care receiver do NOT need to cohabitate in Category 1, but MUST cohabitate in Categories 2 & 3
Family members cannot be hired and paid to provide care to their relative under this program
Care receiver must have documented functional deficits or ADL assistance needs — simply being 60+ is not enough
Reimbursement is determined by sliding scale, not a fixed amount — exact monthly payment varies by household
Caregiver is responsible for paying for services/supplies upfront and tracking expenses for reimbursement — this is not a direct payment program
In-home assessment is mandatory before authorization
Those caring for someone with income above 380% FPL receive no financial assistance but can still access care management, education, and training
programs.data_shape_notes: This program has three distinct eligibility categories with different age, relationship, and cohabitation requirements. Benefits scale by care receiver household income using a sliding scale based on Federal Poverty Level (not fixed tiers). No income eligibility requirement exists, but reimbursement eligibility caps at 380% FPL. Administered regionally through Area Agencies on Aging, but eligibility criteria are statewide. Specific dollar amounts, processing times, and detailed service lists are not available in public search results — families must contact their local AAA for precise benefit amounts.

```json
    "Caregiver and care receiver do NOT need to be related in Category 1 (age 60+ or Alzheimer's), but MUST be related in Categories 2 & 3",
    "Caregiver and care receiver do NOT need to cohabitate in Category 1, but MUST cohabitate in Categories 2 & 3",
    "Family members cannot be hired and paid to provide care to their relative under this program",
    "Care receiver must have documented functional deficits or ADL assistance needs — simply being 60+ is not enough",
    "Reimbursement is determined by sliding scale, not a fixed amount — exact monthly payment varies by household",
    "Caregiver is responsible for paying for services/supplies upfront and tracking expenses for reimbursement — this is not a direct payment program",

    "Those caring for someone with income above 380% FPL receive no financial assistance but can still access care management, education, and training"
  ],
  "data_shape_notes": "This program has three distinct eligibility categories with different age, relationship, and cohabitation requirements. Benefits scale by care receiver household income using a sliding scale based on Federal Poverty Level (not fixed tiers). No income eligibility requirement exists, but reimbursement eligibility caps at 380% FPL. Administered regionally through Area Agencies on Aging, but eligibility criteria are statewide. Specific dollar amounts, processing times, and detailed service lists are not available in public search results — families must contact their local AAA for precise benefit amounts."

```

## Critical Information for Families

**The Three Eligibility Paths**

Pennsylvania's program has three distinct categories, and which one applies dramatically changes the requirements[1][2]:

- **Category 1** (most flexible): Caregiver age 18+, care receiver 60+ OR any age with Alzheimer's/dementia. No relationship required, no cohabitation required.[1][2]
- **Category 2**: Caregiver 55+, care receiver under 18. Caregiver must be related (or have guardianship), and they must live together.[1][2]
- **Category 3**: Caregiver 55+, care receiver 18-59 with disability. Must be related and cohabitate.[1][2]

**Income: The Sliding Scale Trap**

There is no income limit to *qualify*, but income determines *how much you get*[1][2]. If the care receiver's household income exceeds 380% of the Federal Poverty Level, the caregiver receives no financial reimbursement—only non-financial services like care management and training[3]. For those under 380% FPL, reimbursement is calculated on a sliding scale based on household size and income[1][2].

**How Reimbursement Works**

This is not a direct payment program. The caregiver pays for eligible expenses upfront, tracks spending, and submits for reimbursement[7]. The percentage reimbursed depends on the care receiver's household income and size[1][2].

**What You Actually Get**

The search results do not specify which services are reimbursable beyond stating that reimbursement covers "expenses related to providing care"[3]. All caregivers (regardless of income) receive care management, caregiver education, and caregiver training[3]. Specific dollar amounts and service lists require contacting your local Area Agency on Aging[1][2].

**How to Apply**

Contact your county's Area Agency on Aging[1][2]. The search results do not provide specific phone numbers, online application URLs, or form names. An in-home assessment is mandatory before authorization, during which household income must be verified[7].

**What's Missing from Public Information**

The search results do not include: specific monthly reimbursement amounts, processing timelines, waitlist information, specific form names, or detailed lists of reimbursable services. Families must contact their local AAA directly for these details.

programs.name: SCSEP
Legal Counsel for the Elderly

eligibility.income_limits: Pennsylvania legal services for the elderly, such as through SeniorLAW Center or Pennsylvania Bar Association referrals, typically target seniors age 60+ with low income, often aligned with federal poverty guidelines or Medicaid thresholds (e.g., $2,982/month gross for 2026 for community services eligibility; family of four up to $33,075 annually). Exact limits vary by provider and are determined during intake—no fixed statewide table specified.
eligibility.asset_limits: Not explicitly required for most free legal services programs; however, for related Medicaid eligibility counseling, countable assets ≤$2,400 (if income >$2,901/month) or ≤$8,000 (if income ≤$2,982/month in 2026, including $6,000 disregard). Countable assets include most non-excluded resources; exempt typically: primary home (if occupied), one vehicle, personal belongings, burial funds (up to limits).
other_requirements: Pennsylvania residency
Legal issue related to aging (e.g., long-term care planning, elder abuse, guardianship, powers of attorney, Medicaid applications)
Financial need assessed via intake screening

benefits.value: Free legal advice, representation, advocacy, and referrals for elderly-specific issues including long-term care planning, Medicaid eligibility, elder abuse/neglect/exploitation, powers of attorney, guardianship, nursing home rights, housing options, and kinship care (e.g., grandparents raising grandchildren). Provided by attorneys via phone, in-person, or court representation—no fixed dollar amounts or hours.

methods: Phone: 717-236-9486 or 800-322-7572 (Pennsylvania Legal Services intake for eligibility screening)
Phone: 412-261-5555 (Allegheny County Bar Association Lawyer Referral Service)
Online: Visit seniorlawcenter.org and use Service/Staff Locator by county; or pacourts.us for PBA Senior Guide resources
In-person: Local County Bar Association Lawyer Referral Services or Area Agency on Aging offices (locator via paelderlaw.net or seniorlawcenter.org)
application.processing_time: Intake screening immediate via phone; full case acceptance varies (days to weeks based on priority)
application.waitlist: Possible for high-demand areas; priority for urgent issues like abuse or eviction
forms: No specific statewide form; intake via phone or online screener at provider sites
documents: Proof of age (ID)
Proof of income/assets (pay stubs, bank statements, tax returns)
Relevant legal documents (wills, powers of attorney, Medicaid notices)
Details of legal issue (court papers, abuse reports)

geography.restrictions: Available statewide via regional providers; Philadelphia focus via SeniorLAW Center and community legal centers
geography.regional_variations: Delivered through 47 local legal aid offices and county bar referral services; wait times and providers vary (e.g., Allegheny/Pittsburgh: 412-261-5555; Philadelphia: SeniorLAW Center offices)
offices_or_providers: Pennsylvania Legal Services (intake via county locator)
County Bar Associations (e.g., Allegheny, others listed in PBA Guide)

gotchas: Not automatic Medicaid approval—provides legal counsel only; complex asset rules require attorney guidance for spend-down or spousal protections
5-year look-back period for Medicaid applications: improper transfers delay eligibility
Must complete intake screening to confirm low-income eligibility; not all cases accepted
Regional providers handle cases—call local intake first, not direct attorney contact
Separate from general senior services; focuses on legal advocacy, not direct care/funds
programs.data_shape_notes: Delivered via decentralized network of 47 county-based legal aid offices with priority intake; eligibility tied to poverty guidelines without fixed asset test for legal services (unlike Medicaid counseling); varies by county provider availability and caseload
programs._raw:
{
  "name": "Legal Counsel for the Elderly",
  "eligibility": {
    "age": 60,
    "income_limits": "Pennsylvania legal services for the elderly, such as through SeniorLAW Center or Pennsylvania Bar Association referrals, typically target seniors age 60+ with low income, often aligned with federal poverty guidelines or Medicaid thresholds (e.g., $2,982/month gross for 2026 for community services eligibility; family of four up to $33,075 annually). Exact limits vary by provider and are determined during intake—no fixed statewide table specified.",
    "asset_limits": "Not explicitly required for most free legal services programs; however, for related Medicaid eligibility counseling, countable assets ≤$2,400 (if income >$2,901/month) or ≤$8,000 (if income ≤$2,982/month in 2026, including $6,000 disregard). Countable assets include most non-excluded resources; exempt typically: primary home (if occupied), one vehicle, personal belongings, burial funds (up to limits).",
    "other_requirements": ["Pennsylvania residency", "Legal issue related to aging (e.g., long-term care planning, elder abuse, guardianship, powers of attorney, Medicaid applications)", "Financial need assessed via intake screening"]
  },
  "benefits": {
    "type": "service",
    "value": "Free legal advice, representation, advocacy, and referrals for elderly-specific issues including long-term care planning, Medicaid eligibility, elder abuse/neglect/exploitation, powers of attorney, guardianship, nursing home rights, housing options, and kinship care (e.g., grandparents raising grandchildren). Provided by attorneys via phone, in-person, or court representation—no fixed dollar amounts or hours.",
    "varies_by": "priority_tier"
  },
  "application": {
    "methods": [
      "Phone: 717-236-9486 or 800-322-7572 (Pennsylvania Legal Services intake for eligibility screening)",
      "Phone: 412-261-5555 (Allegheny County Bar Association Lawyer Referral Service)",
      "Online: Visit seniorlawcenter.org and use Service/Staff Locator by county; or pacourts.us for PBA Senior Guide resources",
      "In-person: Local County Bar Association Lawyer Referral Services or Area Agency on Aging offices (locator via paelderlaw.net or seniorlawcenter.org)"
    ],
    "processing_time": "Intake screening immediate via phone; full case acceptance varies (days to weeks based on priority)",
    "waitlist": "Possible for high-demand areas; priority for urgent issues like abuse or eviction",
    "forms": ["No specific statewide form; intake via phone or online screener at provider sites"],
    "documents": ["Proof of age (ID)", "Proof of income/assets (pay stubs, bank statements, tax returns)", "Relevant legal documents (wills, powers of attorney, Medicaid notices)", "Details of legal issue (court papers, abuse reports)"]
  },
  "geography": {
    "statewide": true,
    "restrictions": "Available statewide via regional providers; Philadelphia focus via SeniorLAW Center and community legal centers",
    "regional_variations": "Delivered through 47 local legal aid offices and county bar referral services; wait times and providers vary (e.g., Allegheny/Pittsburgh: 412-261-5555; Philadelphia: SeniorLAW Center offices)",
    "offices_or_providers": ["SeniorLAW Center (Philadelphia and statewide referrals)", "Pennsylvania Legal Services (intake via county locator)", "County Bar Associations (e.g., Allegheny, others listed in PBA Guide)"]
  },
  "official_source": "https://seniorlawcenter.org (primary provider); https://www.pacourts.us/Storage/media/pdfs/20251010/170640-pbaseniorguide2025.pdf (PBA Guide)",
  "gotchas": [
    "Not automatic Medicaid approval—provides legal counsel only; complex asset rules require attorney guidance for spend-down or spousal protections",
    "5-year look-back period for Medicaid applications: improper transfers delay eligibility",
    "Must complete intake screening to confirm low-income eligibility; not all cases accepted",
    "Regional providers handle cases—call local intake first, not direct attorney contact",
    "Separate from general senior services; focuses on legal advocacy, not direct care/funds"
  ],
  "data_shape_notes": "Delivered via decentralized network of 47 county-based legal aid offices with priority intake; eligibility tied to poverty guidelines without fixed asset test for legal services (unlike Medicaid counseling); varies by county provider availability and caseload"
}

programs.name: Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits; services are free and available to all regardless of financial status.
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits; no financial tests apply.
other_requirements: Resides (or planning to reside) in a long-term care facility such as nursing homes, assisted living facilities, personal care homes, domiciliary care homes, adult day care centers, or receiving long-term care services in home/community settings.
Complaint or concern related to quality of care, quality of life, rights, discharge/transfer, service disruption, finances, medications, environment, activities, or similar issues in long-term care.
Services available to residents, potential residents, families, friends, staff, or community members on behalf of residents.

benefits.value: Investigation and resolution of complaints; education on rights, procedures, and resources; empowerment through skill-building, resident/family councils, and PEER groups; routine unannounced facility visits; mediation; referrals to long-term care programs; assistance with informed decisions; advocacy for fair treatment, dignity, and quality care. Specific issues addressed: discharge/transfer, service disruption, quality of care/life, residents' finances (e.g., access to funds, Medicare/Medicaid, billing), professional care (medications, nursing, restraints), rights (admission/discharge policies), food/environment/activities.

methods: Phone: Contact local Area Agency on Aging (AAA) Ombudsman program (examples: SWPA AAA for Greene/Fayette/Washington Counties via https://www.swpa-aaa.org/community-services/ombudsman-program; Franklin County via https://www.franklincountypa.gov/departments/ombudsman-program/; CARIE at 215-545-5724 or 610-860-5050 for Philadelphia; Center in the Park at 215-844-1829 for NE/NW Philadelphia; Montgomery County via https://www.montgomerycountypa.gov/2832/Ombudsman).

Statewide assistance: Pennsylvania Department of Aging via https://www.pa.gov/services/aging/request-assistance-from-a-long-term-care-ombudsman or https://www.pa.gov/agencies/aging/aging-programs-and-services/advocacy-education-protection.
In-person: Local AAA offices or facilities (routine visits by ombudsmen).
Email: ombudsman@carie.org (Philadelphia example).
application.processing_time: Immediate response to complaints; no formal processing time specified as services are responsive advocacy.
forms: No specific forms required; contact directly to request assistance.
documents: No documents needed to initiate; provide details of complaint/concern. Resident permission required before discussing with staff (or via POA if resident unable to consent).

geography.restrictions: Services delivered through 52 local Area Agencies on Aging (AAA) covering all Pennsylvania counties.
geography.regional_variations: Administered locally by AAAs with regional offices/providers (e.g., SWPA AAA for Greene/Fayette/Washington Counties[1]; Franklin County Ombudsman[2]; Lancaster County[3]; PCA for Philadelphia[4]; CARIE for parts of Philadelphia, Center in the Park for other Philadelphia areas[7]; Montgomery County[9]). Trained staff, volunteers, or PEER residents serve specific areas. No differences in core services, but contact points and coverage vary by county.
offices_or_providers: Local AAAs; State office: Pennsylvania Department of Aging, 555 Walnut Street, 5th Floor, Harrisburg, PA 17101[10].

gotchas: Not a direct care or financial aid program—provides advocacy only, not healthcare services or funding.
Must be for long-term care settings (facilities or home-based LTC services); not for independent living without LTC involvement.
Confidentiality: Ombudsmen require resident permission (or POA) before intervening with staff[3].
Anyone can contact on behalf of resident (families, friends, staff, public), but priority to resident's wishes.
Not eligibility for facility admission—helps with complaints/rights once in or seeking LTC.
Local variation requires finding correct AAA for your county.
programs.data_shape_notes: no income test; free advocacy services only; county-based delivery via 52 AAAs with no financial eligibility; responsive to complaints rather than pre-qualifying application
